steps.title: Core ADS Components
steps.objective: Identify the fundamental components that make up an ADS system.
keyIdeas: The role of protective devices and the basic requirement for a low-impedance path.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to installation.

ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is a safety strategy designed to cut off power instantly if a fault occurs, preventing electric shock. ADS is not just one part; it is a coordinated system of components working together.

To work correctly, ADS requires two main things: a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow back to the source, and a protective device. The protective device is usually a circuit breaker (MCB), a fuse, or a Residual Current Device (RCD).

When a fault happens, the current flows through the earthing conductors. If the resistance of this path is low enough, the current becomes very high, which triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. This 'auto-off' response is what makes the installation safe for the user.
Explanation A

steps.title: Main and Supplementary Bonding
steps.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
keyIdeas: The specific application of bonding conductors in an installation.
Incorrectly identifying bonding types leads to non-compliant and potentially dangerous installations.
Protective bonding conductors are used to connect metalwork that is not part of the electrical system—such as gas and water pipes—to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).

Main Protective Bonding is the connection made to the incoming services (gas, water, or oil pipes) where they enter the building. Its purpose is to keep all accessible metalwork at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal parts at the same time during a fault.

Supplementary Bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metal parts together, such as a radiator and a metal pipe, within that specific room. While these look like earth wires (green and yellow), their primary job is 'equipotential bonding,' which means ensuring everything stays at the same electrical level to prevent current flowing through a person.
Explanation B
anchorFacts: Protective bonding conductors are used to connect metalwork that is not part of the electrical system—such as gas and water pipes—to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
keyIdeas: The use of the mass of earth as the return path and the necessity of an earth electrode.
TT systems rely on local electrodes rather than a supplier-provided terminal, changing how we test and protect the circuit.

In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for Terre-Terre (the French word for Earth). The first letter 'T' tells us the transformer at the substation is connected directly to the earth. The second letter 'T' tells us the consumer's installation is connected to earth via a local Earth Electrode.

An Earth Electrode is usually a metal rod driven deep into the ground outside the property. In a TT system, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal. Instead, the fault current must travel through the mass of the earth itself to get back to the transformer.

Because soil and rock have higher resistance than copper wire, the fault current in a TT system is often quite low. Because of this, TT systems almost always require an RCD to ensure the power is cut quickly, as a standard fuse or circuit breaker might not detect the small fault current.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters and supply paths.
keyIdeas: Separate (S) vs. Combined (C) neutral and protective conductors in the supply.
The system type dictates the Earth Loop Impedance values and the type of equipment used at the intake.
TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide a high-quality earth return path. 'T' means the source is earthed, and 'N' means the consumer's earth is connected to the Neutral point of the supply.

In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the property. Usually, the earth is the lead sheath or a separate core of the supply cable.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for Combined and the 'S' stands for Separate. In the street, the Neutral and Earth functions are Combined into one single wire (called a PEN conductor). However, once the cable reaches the consumer's house, they are Separated at the intake. This is the most common system for new installations in the UK and is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME).
Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.

steps.title: CPC vs. Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
keyIdeas: The location of the conductor: the Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the means of earthing; the CPC protects individual circuits.
Using the wrong term during inspection and testing leads to failed reports and technical errors.

It is vital to use the correct names for different green-and-yellow conductors. A Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that protects an individual circuit. For example, the earth wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable for a kitchen socket circuit is a CPC. Its job is to link the metal parts of that specific circuit back to the consumer unit.

The Earthing Conductor is the single 'master' wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing. This means it connects the consumer unit to the supplier's earth terminal or to an earth rod.

To remember the difference: the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house, while a CPC is just for one specific circuit. You might have twenty CPCs in a house, but usually only one main Earthing Conductor.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and Components
steps.objective: Identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation based on a provided diagram.
keyIdeas: Identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation based on a provided diagram.
Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are inspecting a small domestic garage conversion. You notice a single concentric supply cable entering the building. At the service head (the cutout), the supplier has provided a single terminal where both the neutral and the earth are connected together. From this point, two separate wires—a blue neutral and a green/yellow earth—run into the consumer unit. You also see a 10mm² green/yellow cable clamped to the copper water main where it enters the building.

Step 1: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a 'Combined' then 'Separate' arrangement. Using our letter codes, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).

Step 2: Identify the main link to the earth source. The green/yellow wire running from the supplier's terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit is the Earthing Conductor. This is the 'master' link for the whole board.

Step 3: Identify the pipe connection. The cable connected to the incoming water main is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. Its job is to ensure the metal pipework stays at the same potential as the electrical earth.

At the service head (the cutout), the supplier has provided a single terminal where both the neutral and the earth are connected together. From this point, two separate wires—a blue neutral and a green/yellow earth—run into the consumer unit. You also see a 10mm² green/yellow cable clamped to the copper water main where it enters the building.

Step 2: Identify the main link to the earth source. The green/yellow wire running from the supplier's terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit is the Earthing Conductor. This is the 'master' link for the whole board.

Step 3: Identify the pipe connection. The cable connected to the incoming water main is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. Its job is to ensure the metal pipework stays at the same potential as the electrical earth.
Step 4: Identify the circuit-level protection. Inside the garage, the green/yellow wire found within the 'twin and earth' cable feeding the lights is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).

steps.title: Practice: Identifying TN-C-S
steps.objective: Identify TN-C-S from the combined-and-separated supply arrangement.
keyIdeas: TN-C-S combines neutral and earth on part of the supply.
The conductor is separated into neutral and earth at the service position.
anchorFacts: A PEN conductor means protective earth and neutral are combined.
TN-C-S is also known as PME.
taskConstraints: Keep the scenario focused on combined then separated supply conductors.
Require one earthing-system answer only.
steps.questionIntent: Identify TN-C-S from a combined then separated supply arrangement.

taskSkeleton.scenario: The supply neutral and earth are combined in one conductor outside the installation and separated at the service position.
steps: Identify the earthing system from the supply arrangement.

steps.questionText: Which earthing system is described in this scenario?
steps.hint: Combined first, then separated, points to TN-C-S.

steps.title: Integrative Close: Why TT Uses an RCD
steps.objective: Explain why TT systems usually need an RCD.
keyIdeas: TT systems rely on soil for the return path, which has much higher resistance than a metal return path.
High earth path resistance limits fault current, so a fuse or MCB may not trip quickly enough.
An RCD is used because it trips on imbalance rather than needing a large fault current.
anchorFacts: High soil resistance means low fault current in a TT system.
An RCD can disconnect even when the fault current is too small for a fuse or MCB to operate quickly.
taskConstraints: Keep the response to one short explanation.
Require the learner to link high resistance to low fault current and then to RCD operation.
steps.questionIntent: Explain why TT systems usually require an RCD.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A TT installation depends on a local earth electrode and the earth path is relatively high resistance.
steps: Explain why this makes an RCD necessary.

steps.questionText: Using one short explanation, explain why TT systems usually require an RCD.
answerGuidance: fault current too low, high resistance earth path, and RCD trips on imbalance
steps.hint: The key issue is not the label TT by itself; it is the resistance of the earth return path.

steps.title: Core ADS Components
steps.objective: Identify the fundamental components that make up an ADS system.
keyIdeas: The role of protective devices and the basic requirement for a low-impedance path.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock.
ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is a safety strategy designed to cut off power instantly if a fault occurs.
To work correctly, ADS requires a low-resistance path for the fault current and a protective device (MCB, fuse, or RCD).
When a fault happens, the current flows through the earthing conductors. If the resistance is low enough, the current becomes high enough to trigger the protective device.
anchorFacts: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.
ADS requires a low-resistance path for fault current and a protective device (MCB, fuse, or RCD).
The protective device 'trips' or blows when fault current is high enough, making the installation safe.
ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply) is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
Circuit Protective Conductor is the correct expansion for CPC.
Automatic Disconnection of Supply is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
Circuit Protective Conductor is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking ADS is a single device rather than a system of coordinated components.
Think of ADS as a team effort between the wires that carry the fault and the device that switches off the power.
taskConstraints: No mention of Zs or Ze values.
Do not drift into: Exposed conductive parts.
Do not drift into: Extraneous conductive parts.
steps.questionIntent: The role of protective devices and the basic requirement for a low-impedance path.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to installation.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does ADS stand for?
What does the abbreviation CPC stand for?
What general term is used for the device (like a fuse or MCB) that disconnects the supply during a fault?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What are the main reasons the fault path must have low enough resistance for ADS to work correctly?
deeperAnswerGuidance: So enough fault current flows
So the protective device trips quickly
So the supply disconnects safely
steps.hint: ADS needs a complete fault path and a protective device that can operate quickly.

steps.title: Main and Supplementary Bonding
steps.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
keyIdeas: Protective bonding conductors connect metalwork that is not part of the electrical system—such as gas and water pipes—to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
Main Protective Bonding is the connection made to the incoming services (gas, water, or oil pipes) where they enter the building.
The purpose of bonding is to keep all accessible metalwork at the same voltage (potential) to prevent electric shock.
Supplementary Bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms to connect local metal parts together.
anchorFacts: Protective bonding conductors connect non-electrical metalwork (gas/water pipes) to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
Main Protective Bonding is the connection made to incoming services where they enter the building.
Supplementary Bonding is used in high-risk areas like bathrooms to connect local metal parts.
Main Protective Bonding is the connection made to incoming gas or water pipes where they first enter a building.
Equipotential zone is the technical term for the zone where accessible metalwork is kept at the same voltage.
misconceptionsToWatch: Believing all green/yellow wires perform the same function regardless of where they are connected.
Main bonding is for the whole building's incoming pipes; supplementary is for extra safety in specific rooms like bathrooms.
taskConstraints: Do not use the term 'Extraneous conductive parts' yet as it is out of scope.
Focus on the items (gas/water pipes) rather than the technical classification.
steps.questionIntent: The specific application of bonding conductors in an installation.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Incorrectly identifying bonding types leads to non-compliant and potentially dangerous installations.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the technical term for the zone where accessible metalwork is kept at the same voltage?
What items, such as gas or water pipes, are connected by main protective bonding?
To which terminal are bonding conductors connected?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What are the main reasons bonding does not replace the CPC during a fault?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Because the CPC carries fault current
Bonding keeps metalwork at the same potential
Bonding is not the main fault path
steps.hint: Bonding is for equal potential; the CPC is the fault-current path.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
keyIdeas: In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for Terre-Terre (French for Earth).
The first 'T' means the transformer is earthed; the second 'T' means the consumer's installation is earthed via a local Earth Electrode.
An Earth Electrode is usually a metal rod driven into the ground because the supplier does not provide an earth terminal.
TT systems usually require an RCD because soil resistance is higher than copper, leading to lower fault currents.
anchorFacts: In a TT system, the consumer's installation is connected to earth via a local Earth Electrode.
The fault current in a TT system travels through the mass of the earth itself to get back to the transformer.
TT systems almost always require an RCD to ensure quick disconnection due to high earth resistance.
Terre is the correct expansion for the letter T in these systems.
TT is the system that uses a local earth rod.
TT is the correct technical answer for this question.
misconceptionsToWatch: Assuming TT systems do not have a return path to the transformer.
Remember: T stands for Terre (Earth). In a TT system, both the source and the house have their own separate connection to the ground.
taskConstraints: Standardize on 'Terre' as per BS 7671 convention.
steps.questionIntent: The use of the mass of earth as the return path and the necessity of an earth electrode.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: TT systems rely on local electrodes rather than a supplier-provided terminal, changing how we test and protect the circuit.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which earthing system uses a local earth electrode at the installation?
Which protective device is usually required on a TT system because the earth path is high resistance?
Who is responsible for providing the earth electrode in a TT system?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What are the main reasons a TT system usually needs an RCD instead of relying only on a fuse or MCB?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Fault current is too low
Earth path resistance is high
An RCD can trip on small imbalance current
steps.hint: TT relies on the local earth, so the fault path is usually higher resistance.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters and supply paths.
keyIdeas: TN systems use the supplier's cable for a high-quality earth return path.
In TN-S, 'S' stands for Separate. Neutral and Earth are separate wires from the transformer to the property.
In TN-C-S, 'C' stands for Combined and 'S' stands for Separate. Neutral and Earth are combined in the street (PEN conductor) but separated at the intake.
TN-C-S is often called Protective Multiple Earthing (PME).
anchorFacts: TN-S uses separate neutral and earth conductors throughout the supply.
TN-C-S uses a combined neutral and earth conductor (PEN) in the supply, which is separated at the intake.
PME is the common name for a TN-C-S system where the supplier provides the earth terminal.
Separate is the correct expansion for the 'S' in TN-S.
Combined is the correct expansion for the 'C' in TN-C-S.
Separate is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
Combined is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
misconceptionsToWatch: Confusing the 'S' in TN-S with 'Supply' rather than 'Separate'.
Focus on the last letters: S means Separate wires all the way; C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate.
taskConstraints: Ensure the guidance only contains the exact technical term 'Combined' for the abbreviation check.
steps.questionIntent: Separate (S) vs. Combined (C) neutral and protective conductors in the supply.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: The system type dictates the Earth Loop Impedance values and the type of equipment used at the intake.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the letter S stand for in TN-S?
What does the letter C stand for in TN-C-S?
What is the technical name for the conductor that combines protective earth and neutral on the supply side?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is TN-C-S described as a system where neutral and earth are combined first and then separated?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Because the supply uses a PEN conductor first, Because earth and neutral are combined on part of the supply, and They are separated at the service position
steps.hint: S = Separate, C = Combined, PEN = Protective Earth and Neutral.

steps.title: CPC vs. Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
keyIdeas: A Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) protects an individual circuit (e.g., the earth wire in a socket circuit).
The Earthing Conductor is the single 'master' wire connecting the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (rod or supplier terminal).
You may have many CPCs in a building, but usually only one main Earthing Conductor.
anchorFacts: A CPC links the metal parts of a specific circuit back to the consumer unit.
The Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the actual means of earthing.
The Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole installation.
CPC stands for Circuit Protective Conductor.
Circuit Protective Conductor is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.
misconceptionsToWatch: Using the terms 'CPC' and 'Earthing Conductor' interchangeably.
The Earthing Conductor is the 'big' main wire at the intake; the CPC is the 'small' wire inside the cables going to your lights and sockets.
taskConstraints: Ensure answer guidance for the 'where' question points to the MET or Consumer Unit.
steps.questionIntent: The location of the conductor: the Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the means of earthing; the CPC protects individual circuits.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Using the wrong term during inspection and testing leads to failed reports and technical errors.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation CPC stand for?
Which conductor connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing, such as an earth rod?

steps.deeperQuestionText: If an installation has twenty individual circuits, why is there usually only one Earthing Conductor but twenty CPCs?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The Earthing Conductor is the master link for the whole house
Each individual circuit requires its own CPC
CPCs protect specific circuit runs
The Earthing Conductor serves the entire distribution board
steps.hint: The Earthing Conductor is the 'big' main wire at the intake; the CPC is the 'small' wire inside the cables going to your lights and sockets.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.
- anchorPhrases should be reusable phrases that later phases can teach verbatim or near-verbatim.

terms.simpleDefinition: A safety strategy that automatically cuts off power during a fault to prevent electric shock.
terms.misconception: Thinking ADS is just a single fuse rather than a coordinated system of conductors and devices.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the installation's earth connection is made through a local electrode in the ground.
terms.anchor: Earth via a rod in the dirt.
terms.misconception: Assuming TT systems have no return path to the transformer; they use the mass of earth as the path.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the neutral and earth conductors are separate throughout the entire supply.
terms.anchor: Separate neutral and earth all the way.
terms.misconception: Believing the 'S' stands for 'Supply' rather than 'Separate'.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated at the intake.
terms.misconception: Confusing it with TN-S because both provide an earth terminal to the consumer.

terms.simpleDefinition: The conductor connecting the metal parts of a specific circuit to the main earthing terminal.
terms.anchor: The earth wire inside a circuit cable.
terms.misconception: Using the term 'CPC' to describe the main wire that connects the whole house to the earth source.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main conductor connecting the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (e.g., a rod or supplier terminal).
terms.anchor: The master link between the installation and the earth source.
terms.misconception: Thinking this is the same as a CPC; the Earthing Conductor serves the whole board, not just one circuit.

terms.simpleDefinition: Conductors used to connect metal services like gas and water pipes to the Main Earthing Terminal.
terms.anchor: Connecting the pipes to the MET.
terms.misconception: Believing bonding is meant to carry fault current like a CPC; its job is to keep metalwork at the same voltage.

terms.simpleDefinition: A metal rod or plate driven into the ground to provide a physical connection to the earth.
terms.anchor: The physical rod in the ground.
terms.misconception: Thinking every house has one; they are primarily required for TT systems.

terms.simpleDefinition: A component such as a circuit breaker (MCB) or RCD that detects a fault and disconnects the supply.
terms.anchor: The device that trips to stop the flow of electricity.
terms.misconception: Assuming all protective devices protect against both shock and fire equally.

anchorPhrases: ADS requires a low-resistance path to work
The Earthing Conductor is the 'big green wire' at the MET
misconceptionTargets: Interchanging the terms CPC and Earthing Conductor
Confusing the function of bonding with the function of earthing
Misinterpreting the letter codes in earthing system types
Believing ADS is a single component rather than a system

teachChecks.title: Core ADS Components
teachChecks.objective: Identify the fundamental components that make up an ADS system.
retrievalTextLines: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is a safety strategy designed to cut off power instantly if a fault occurs, preventing electric shock. ADS is not just one part; it is a coordinated system of components working together.

To work correctly, ADS requires two main things: a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow back to the source, and a protective device. The protective device is usually a circuit breaker (MCB), a fuse, or a Residual Current Device (RCD).

When a fault happens, the current flows through the earthing conductors. If the resistance of this path is low enough, the current becomes very high, which triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. This 'auto-off' response is what makes the installation safe for the user.
teachChecks.hint: Think of ADS as a team effort between the wires that carry the fault and the device that switches off the power.

teachChecks.title: Main and Supplementary Bonding
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
retrievalTextLines: Protective bonding conductors are used to connect metalwork that is not part of the electrical system—such as gas and water pipes—to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).

Main Protective Bonding is the connection made to the incoming services (gas, water, or oil pipes) where they enter the building. Its purpose is to keep all accessible metalwork at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal parts at the same time during a fault.

Supplementary Bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metal parts together, such as a radiator and a metal pipe, within that specific room. While these look like earth wires (green and yellow), their primary job is 'equipotential bonding,' which means ensuring everything stays at the same electrical level to prevent current flowing through a person.
teachChecks.hint: Main bonding is for the whole building's incoming pipes; supplementary is for extra safety in specific rooms like bathrooms.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems
teachChecks.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
retrievalTextLines: In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for Terre-Terre (the French word for Earth). The first letter 'T' tells us the transformer at the substation is connected directly to the earth. The second letter 'T' tells us the consumer's installation is connected to earth via a local Earth Electrode.

An Earth Electrode is usually a metal rod driven deep into the ground outside the property. In a TT system, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal. Instead, the fault current must travel through the mass of the earth itself to get back to the transformer.

Because soil and rock have higher resistance than copper wire, the fault current in a TT system is often quite low. Because of this, TT systems almost always require an RCD to ensure the power is cut quickly, as a standard fuse or circuit breaker might not detect the small fault current.
teachChecks.hint: Remember: T stands for Terre (Earth). In a TT system, both the source and the house have their own separate connection to the ground.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters and supply paths.
retrievalTextLines: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide a high-quality earth return path. 'T' means the source is earthed, and 'N' means the consumer's earth is connected to the Neutral point of the supply.

In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the property. Usually, the earth is the lead sheath or a separate core of the supply cable.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for Combined and the 'S' stands for Separate. In the street, the Neutral and Earth functions are Combined into one single wire (called a PEN conductor). However, once the cable reaches the consumer's house, they are Separated at the intake. This is the most common system for new installations in the UK and is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME).
teachChecks.hint: Focus on the last letters: S means Separate wires all the way; C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate.

teachChecks.title: CPC vs. Earthing Conductor
teachChecks.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
retrievalTextLines: It is vital to use the correct names for different green-and-yellow conductors. A Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that protects an individual circuit. For example, the earth wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable for a kitchen socket circuit is a CPC. Its job is to link the metal parts of that specific circuit back to the consumer unit.

The Earthing Conductor is the single 'master' wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing. This means it connects the consumer unit to the supplier's earth terminal or to an earth rod.

To remember the difference: the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house, while a CPC is just for one specific circuit. You might have twenty CPCs in a house, but usually only one main Earthing Conductor.
teachChecks.hint: The Earthing Conductor is the 'big' main wire at the intake; the CPC is the 'small' wire inside the cables going to your lights and sockets.

- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.

deeperAnswerGuidance: Keeps all metalwork at the same voltage

Explanation output:
{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "Core ADS Components",
      "objective": "Identify the fundamental components that make up an ADS system.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is a safety strategy designed to cut off power instantly if a fault occurs, preventing electric shock. ADS is not just one part; it is a coordinated system of components working together.",
        "To work correctly, ADS requires two main things: a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow back to the source, and a protective device. The protective device is usually a circuit breaker (MCB), a fuse, or a Residual Current Device (RCD).",
        "When a fault happens, the current flows through the earthing conductors. If the resistance of this path is low enough, the current becomes very high, which triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. This 'auto-off' response is what makes the installation safe for the user."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of ADS as a team effort between the wires that carry the fault and the device that switches off the power."
    },
    {
      "title": "Main and Supplementary Bonding",
      "objective": "Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Protective bonding conductors are used to connect metalwork that is not part of the electrical system—such as gas and water pipes—to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).",
        "Main Protective Bonding is the connection made to the incoming services (gas, water, or oil pipes) where they enter the building. Its purpose is to keep all accessible metalwork at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal parts at the same time during a fault.",
        "Supplementary Bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metal parts together, such as a radiator and a metal pipe, within that specific room. While these look like earth wires (green and yellow), their primary job is 'equipotential bonding,' which means ensuring everything stays at the same electrical level to prevent current flowing through a person."
      ],
      "hint": "Main bonding is for the whole building's incoming pipes; supplementary is for extra safety in specific rooms like bathrooms."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for Terre-Terre (the French word for Earth). The first letter 'T' tells us the transformer at the substation is connected directly to the earth. The second letter 'T' tells us the consumer's installation is connected to earth via a local Earth Electrode.",
        "An Earth Electrode is usually a metal rod driven deep into the ground outside the property. In a TT system, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal. Instead, the fault current must travel through the mass of the earth itself to get back to the transformer.",
        "Because soil and rock have higher resistance than copper wire, the fault current in a TT system is often quite low. Because of this, TT systems almost always require an RCD to ensure the power is cut quickly, as a standard fuse or circuit breaker might not detect the small fault current."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember: T stands for Terre (Earth). In a TT system, both the source and the house have their own separate connection to the ground."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters and supply paths.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide a high-quality earth return path. 'T' means the source is earthed, and 'N' means the consumer's earth is connected to the Neutral point of the supply.",
        "In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the property. Usually, the earth is the lead sheath or a separate core of the supply cable.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for Combined and the 'S' stands for Separate. In the street, the Neutral and Earth functions are Combined into one single wire (called a PEN conductor). However, once the cable reaches the consumer's house, they are Separated at the intake. This is the most common system for new installations in the UK and is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME)."
      ],
      "hint": "Focus on the last letters: S means Separate wires all the way; C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC vs. Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "It is vital to use the correct names for different green-and-yellow conductors. A Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that protects an individual circuit. For example, the earth wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable for a kitchen socket circuit is a CPC. Its job is to link the metal parts of that specific circuit back to the consumer unit.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the single 'master' wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing. This means it connects the consumer unit to the supplier's earth terminal or to an earth rod.",
        "To remember the difference: the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house, while a CPC is just for one specific circuit. You might have twenty CPCs in a house, but usually only one main Earthing Conductor."
      ],
      "hint": "The Earthing Conductor is the 'big' main wire at the intake; the CPC is the 'small' wire inside the cables going to your lights and sockets."
    }
  ]
}

  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are inspecting a small domestic garage conversion. You notice a single concentric supply cable entering the building. At the service head (the cutout), the supplier has provided a single terminal where both the neutral and the earth are connected together. From this point, two separate wires—a blue neutral and a green/yellow earth—run into the consumer unit. You also see a 10mm² green/yellow cable clamped to the copper water main where it enters the building.",
    "Step 1: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a 'Combined' then 'Separate' arrangement. Using our letter codes, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).",
    "Step 2: Identify the main link to the earth source. The green/yellow wire running from the supplier's terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit is the Earthing Conductor. This is the 'master' link for the whole board.",
    "Step 3: Identify the pipe connection. The cable connected to the incoming water main is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. Its job is to ensure the metal pipework stays at the same potential as the electrical earth.",
    "Step 4: Identify the circuit-level protection. Inside the garage, the green/yellow wire found within the 'twin and earth' cable feeding the lights is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).",
    "Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S earthing system. The key ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, the Main Protective Bonding Conductor, and the CPCs for the individual circuits.",
    "Takeaway: Always look at the 'intake' (where the power enters) to determine the system type. If neutral and earth share a terminal at the cutout but split before the meter, it is TN-C-S. Don't confuse the Earthing Conductor (the main link) with the CPC (the circuit link)."
  ],
  "hint": "Focus on the letters: C stands for Combined (in the street) and S stands for Separate (in the house)."
}

parsed.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and Components
parsed.objective: Identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation based on a provided diagram.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are inspecting a small domestic garage conversion. You notice a single concentric supply cable entering the building. At the service head (the cutout), the supplier has provided a single terminal where both the neutral and the earth are connected together. From this point, two separate wires—a blue neutral and a green/yellow earth—run into the consumer unit. You also see a 10mm² green/yellow cable clamped to the copper water main where it enters the building.

Step 1: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a 'Combined' then 'Separate' arrangement. Using our letter codes, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).

Step 2: Identify the main link to the earth source. The green/yellow wire running from the supplier's terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit is the Earthing Conductor. This is the 'master' link for the whole board.

Step 3: Identify the pipe connection. The cable connected to the incoming water main is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. Its job is to ensure the metal pipework stays at the same potential as the electrical earth.
Step 4: Identify the circuit-level protection. Inside the garage, the green/yellow wire found within the 'twin and earth' cable feeding the lights is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).

Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S earthing system. The key ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, the Main Protective Bonding Conductor, and the CPCs for the individual circuits.

Takeaway: Always look at the 'intake' (where the power enters) to determine the system type. If neutral and earth share a terminal at the cutout but split before the meter, it is TN-C-S. Don't confuse the Earthing Conductor (the main link) with the CPC (the circuit link).
parsed.hint: Focus on the letters: C stands for Combined (in the street) and S stands for Separate (in the house).

Grounded source text:
Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.

Vocabulary output:
{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply)",
      "simpleDefinition": "A safety strategy that automatically cuts off power during a fault to prevent electric shock.",
      "anchor": "The system's 'auto-off' safety response.",
      "misconception": "Thinking ADS is just a single fuse rather than a coordinated system of conductors and devices."
    },
    {
      "term": "TT System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where the installation's earth connection is made through a local electrode in the ground.",
      "anchor": "Earth via a rod in the dirt.",
      "misconception": "Assuming TT systems have no return path to the transformer; they use the mass of earth as the path."
    },
    {
      "term": "TN-S System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where the neutral and earth conductors are separate throughout the entire supply.",
      "anchor": "Separate neutral and earth all the way.",
      "misconception": "Believing the 'S' stands for 'Supply' rather than 'Separate'."
    },
    {
      "term": "TN-C-S System",
      "simpleDefinition": "An earthing arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated at the intake.",
      "anchor": "Combined then Separated.",
      "misconception": "Confusing it with TN-S because both provide an earth terminal to the consumer."
    },
    {
      "term": "CPC (Circuit Protective Conductor)",
      "simpleDefinition": "The conductor connecting the metal parts of a specific circuit to the main earthing terminal.",
      "anchor": "The earth wire inside a circuit cable.",
      "misconception": "Using the term 'CPC' to describe the main wire that connects the whole house to the earth source."
    },
    {
      "term": "Earthing Conductor",
      "simpleDefinition": "The main conductor connecting the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (e.g., a rod or supplier terminal).",
      "anchor": "The master link between the installation and the earth source.",
      "misconception": "Thinking this is the same as a CPC; the Earthing Conductor serves the whole board, not just one circuit."
    },
    {
      "term": "Main Protective Bonding",
      "simpleDefinition": "Conductors used to connect metal services like gas and water pipes to the Main Earthing Terminal.",
      "anchor": "Connecting the pipes to the MET.",
      "misconception": "Believing bonding is meant to carry fault current like a CPC; its job is to keep metalwork at the same voltage."
    },
    {
      "term": "Earth Electrode",
      "simpleDefinition": "A metal rod or plate driven into the ground to provide a physical connection to the earth.",
      "anchor": "The physical rod in the ground.",
      "misconception": "Thinking every house has one; they are primarily required for TT systems."
    },
    {
      "term": "Protective Device",
      "simpleDefinition": "A component such as a circuit breaker (MCB) or RCD that detects a fault and disconnects the supply.",
      "anchor": "The device that trips to stop the flow of electricity.",
      "misconception": "Assuming all protective devices protect against both shock and fire equally."
    }
  ],

Worked example output:
{
  "title": "Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and Components",
  "objective": "Identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation based on a provided diagram.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are inspecting a small domestic garage conversion. You notice a single concentric supply cable entering the building. At the service head (the cutout), the supplier has provided a single terminal where both the neutral and the earth are connected together. From this point, two separate wires—a blue neutral and a green/yellow earth—run into the consumer unit. You also see a 10mm² green/yellow cable clamped to the copper water main where it enters the building.",
    "Step 1: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a 'Combined' then 'Separate' arrangement. Using our letter codes, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).",
    "Step 2: Identify the main link to the earth source. The green/yellow wire running from the supplier's terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit is the Earthing Conductor. This is the 'master' link for the whole board.",
    "Step 3: Identify the pipe connection. The cable connected to the incoming water main is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. Its job is to ensure the metal pipework stays at the same potential as the electrical earth.",
    "Step 4: Identify the circuit-level protection. Inside the garage, the green/yellow wire found within the 'twin and earth' cable feeding the lights is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).",
    "Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S earthing system. The key ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, the Main Protective Bonding Conductor, and the CPCs for the individual circuits.",
    "Takeaway: Always look at the 'intake' (where the power enters) to determine the system type. If neutral and earth share a terminal at the cutout but split before the meter, it is TN-C-S. Don't confuse the Earthing Conductor (the main link) with the CPC (the circuit link)."
  ],
  "hint": "Focus on the letters: C stands for Combined (in the street) and S stands for Separate (in the house)."
}

- guidedPractice retrieval and task must clearly mirror the worked example pattern.
- practice must be more independent than guidedPractice.
- Do not use these phases to smuggle in extra untaught chunk content that should have been its own teach/check section.
- Each phase must contain one coherent task with one response target type.
- Do not bundle several unrelated sub-questions, several scenarios, or mixed outputs into a single response field.
- Do not write identify-plus-explain bundles in one response box. If synthesis is needed, rewrite as one coherent explanation task.
- Do not ask for long ordered lists, “identify all of these”, or “answer 1, 2, 3” style tasks in these phases.
- If the learner must compare two items, the question must still produce one coherent explanation, not two separate hidden marking jobs.
- If a task currently asks for one conversion plus one classification, rewrite it so the learner gives one combined markable answer string or reduce it to one single target.
- answerGuidance must map directly to the exact response expected from that one task.
- If a task asks for a specific technical term or named item, answerGuidance must prioritise the exact target only.
- answerGuidance arrays must be concrete and gradeable.
- Never return empty questionText, empty retrievalTextLines, or empty answerGuidance for guidedPractice or practice.
- If you are unsure, generate a narrower single-target task instead of leaving any field blank.

{
  "guidedPractice": {
    "title": "Guided Practice",
    "objective": "Identify the earthing system type and the specific earth-source component in a rural installation scenario.",
    "retrievalTextLines": [
      "Scenario: You are working on a remote farm building. The supply arrives via two overhead lines (Line and Neutral).",
      "The supplier has not provided an earth terminal at the cutout.",
      "To provide an earth, you have installed a 16mm² green/yellow cable running from the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to a copper-coated steel rod driven into the soil outside.",
      "Step 1: Look at the source of earth. Since it is a local rod in the ground (Terre) and not provided by the supplier, this is a TT system.",
      "Step 2: Identify the component in the ground. A rod or plate used to connect the installation to the mass of earth is an Earth Electrode."
    ],
    "questionText": "Based on the scenario provided, identify the earthing system type and the technical name for the metal rod driven into the ground.",
    "answerGuidance": [
      "TT system",
      "Earth electrode"
    ],
    "hint": "Remember that 'T' stands for Terre (Earth). If the earth comes from a rod in the dirt rather than the supplier's cable, it is a TT arrangement."
  },
  "practice": {
    "title": "Practice",
    "objective": "Identify earthing systems from technical descriptions and distinguish between main and circuit-level conductors.",
    "retrievalTextLines": [
      "System codes: T (Terre/Earth), N (Neutral), S (Separate), C (Combined).",
      "The Earthing Conductor is the main link from the MET to the earth source.",
      "The CPC (Circuit Protective Conductor) is the specific wire protecting an individual circuit."
    ],
    "questionText": "Identify the earthing system where the neutral and earth conductors are kept separate (S) throughout the entire supply, and name the specific conductor used to connect the metal casing of a single kitchen appliance circuit back to the consumer unit.",
    "answerGuidance": [
      "TN-S",
      "CPC",
      "Circuit Protective Conductor"
    ],
    "hint": "Focus on the letter 'S' for 'Separate'. For the conductor, think about which one protects the 'Circuit' specifically."
  }
}

guidedPractice.title: Guided Practice
guidedPractice.objective: Identify the earthing system type and the specific earth-source component in a rural installation scenario.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are working on a remote farm building. The supply arrives via two overhead lines (Line and Neutral).
The supplier has not provided an earth terminal at the cutout.
To provide an earth, you have installed a 16mm² green/yellow cable running from the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to a copper-coated steel rod driven into the soil outside.
Step 1: Look at the source of earth. Since it is a local rod in the ground (Terre) and not provided by the supplier, this is a TT system.
Step 2: Identify the component in the ground. A rod or plate used to connect the installation to the mass of earth is an Earth Electrode.
guidedPractice.questionText: Based on the scenario provided, identify the earthing system type and the technical name for the metal rod driven into the ground.
guidedPractice.hint: Remember that 'T' stands for Terre (Earth). If the earth comes from a rod in the dirt rather than the supplier's cable, it is a TT arrangement.

practice.title: Practice
practice.objective: Identify earthing systems from technical descriptions and distinguish between main and circuit-level conductors.
retrievalTextLines: System codes: T (Terre/Earth), N (Neutral), S (Separate), C (Combined).
The Earthing Conductor is the main link from the MET to the earth source.
The CPC (Circuit Protective Conductor) is the specific wire protecting an individual circuit.
practice.questionText: Identify the earthing system where the neutral and earth conductors are kept separate (S) throughout the entire supply, and name the specific conductor used to connect the metal casing of a single kitchen appliance circuit back to the consumer unit.
practice.hint: Focus on the letter 'S' for 'Separate'. For the conductor, think about which one protects the 'Circuit' specifically.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.

{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "Core ADS Components",
      "objective": "Identify the fundamental components that make up an ADS system.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is a safety strategy designed to cut off power instantly if a fault occurs, preventing electric shock. ADS is not just one part; it is a coordinated system of components working together.",
        "To work correctly, ADS requires two main things: a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow back to the source, and a protective device. The protective device is usually a circuit breaker (MCB), a fuse, or a Residual Current Device (RCD).",
        "When a fault happens, the current flows through the earthing conductors. If the resistance of this path is low enough, the current becomes very high, which triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. This 'auto-off' response is what makes the installation safe for the user."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of ADS as a team effort between the wires that carry the fault and the device that switches off the power."
    },
    {
      "title": "Main and Supplementary Bonding",
      "objective": "Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Protective bonding conductors are used to connect metalwork that is not part of the electrical system—such as gas and water pipes—to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).",
        "Main Protective Bonding is the connection made to the incoming services (gas, water, or oil pipes) where they enter the building. Its purpose is to keep all accessible metalwork at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal parts at the same time during a fault.",
        "Supplementary Bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metal parts together, such as a radiator and a metal pipe, within that specific room. While these look like earth wires (green and yellow), their primary job is 'equipotential bonding,' which means ensuring everything stays at the same electrical level to prevent current flowing through a person."
      ],
      "hint": "Main bonding is for the whole building's incoming pipes; supplementary is for extra safety in specific rooms like bathrooms."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for Terre-Terre (the French word for Earth). The first letter 'T' tells us the transformer at the substation is connected directly to the earth. The second letter 'T' tells us the consumer's installation is connected to earth via a local Earth Electrode.",
        "An Earth Electrode is usually a metal rod driven deep into the ground outside the property. In a TT system, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal. Instead, the fault current must travel through the mass of the earth itself to get back to the transformer.",
        "Because soil and rock have higher resistance than copper wire, the fault current in a TT system is often quite low. Because of this, TT systems almost always require an RCD to ensure the power is cut quickly, as a standard fuse or circuit breaker might not detect the small fault current."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember: T stands for Terre (Earth). In a TT system, both the source and the house have their own separate connection to the ground."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters and supply paths.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide a high-quality earth return path. 'T' means the source is earthed, and 'N' means the consumer's earth is connected to the Neutral point of the supply.",
        "In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the property. Usually, the earth is the lead sheath or a separate core of the supply cable.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for Combined and the 'S' stands for Separate. In the street, the Neutral and Earth functions are Combined into one single wire (called a PEN conductor). However, once the cable reaches the consumer's house, they are Separated at the intake. This is the most common system for new installations in the UK and is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME)."
      ],
      "hint": "Focus on the last letters: S means Separate wires all the way; C-S means they start Combined and then become Separate."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC vs. Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "It is vital to use the correct names for different green-and-yellow conductors. A Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that protects an individual circuit. For example, the earth wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable for a kitchen socket circuit is a CPC. Its job is to link the metal parts of that specific circuit back to the consumer unit.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the single 'master' wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing. This means it connects the consumer unit to the supplier's earth terminal or to an earth rod.",
        "To remember the difference: the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house, while a CPC is just for one specific circuit. You might have twenty CPCs in a house, but usually only one main Earthing Conductor."
      ],
      "hint": "The Earthing Conductor is the 'big' main wire at the intake; the CPC is the 'small' wire inside the cables going to your lights and sockets."
    }
  ]
}

integrative.title: Integrative Close
integrative.objective: Synthesize the relationship between earthing system arrangements and the specific conductors used in the ADS path.
retrievalTextLines: To achieve Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS), a fault current must follow a continuous path from the point of the fault back to the transformer.
This path relies on the specific arrangement of the supply (TT, TN-S, or TN-C-S) and the correct installation of both circuit-level and main-level conductors.
integrative.questionText: A property is supplied by a cable where the neutral and earth functions are combined in the street but separated into two distinct terminals at the consumer's intake. Describe the path a fault current takes from a metal appliance casing back to the supplier's earth terminal, specifically naming the earthing system type and the two different green-and-yellow conductors the current must pass through.
answerGuidance: The system is a TN-C-S arrangement (or PME).
The fault current first travels from the appliance to the consumer unit via the CPC (Circuit Protective Conductor).
The current then travels from the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the supplier's earth terminal via the Earthing Conductor.
integrative.hint: Think about the letter codes for 'Combined' then 'Separate', and remember the difference between the wire for a single circuit and the 'master' wire for the whole board.

steps.title: Core ADS Components
steps.objective: Identify the fundamental components that make up an ADS system.
keyIdeas: The role of protective devices and the basic requirement for a low-impedance path.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to installation.

ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is a safety strategy designed to cut off power instantly if a fault occurs, preventing electric shock. ADS is not just one part; it is a coordinated system of components working together.

To work correctly, ADS requires two main things: a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow back to the source, and a protective device. The protective device is usually a circuit breaker (MCB), a fuse, or a Residual Current Device (RCD).

When a fault happens, the current flows through the earthing conductors. If the resistance of this path is low enough, the current becomes very high, which triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. This 'auto-off' response is what makes the installation safe for the user.
Explanation A
anchorFacts: ADS stands for Automatic Disconnection of Supply. It is a safety strategy designed to cut off power instantly if a fault occurs, preventing electric shock. ADS is not just one part; it is a coordinated system of components working together.

To work correctly, ADS requires two main things: a low-resistance path for the fault current to flow back to the source, and a protective device. The protective device is usually a circuit breaker (MCB), a fuse, or a Residual Current Device (RCD).

When a fault happens, the current flows through the earthing conductors. If the resistance of this path is low enough, the current becomes very high, which triggers the protective device to 'trip' or blow. This 'auto-off' response is what makes the installation safe for the user.
ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply) is the correct expansion for this abbreviation.

steps.title: Main and Supplementary Bonding
steps.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
keyIdeas: The specific application of bonding conductors in an installation.
Incorrectly identifying bonding types leads to non-compliant and potentially dangerous installations.
Protective bonding conductors are used to connect metalwork that is not part of the electrical system—such as gas and water pipes—to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).

Main Protective Bonding is the connection made to the incoming services (gas, water, or oil pipes) where they enter the building. Its purpose is to keep all accessible metalwork at the same voltage (potential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal parts at the same time during a fault.

Supplementary Bonding is additional bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metal parts together, such as a radiator and a metal pipe, within that specific room. While these look like earth wires (green and yellow), their primary job is 'equipotential bonding,' which means ensuring everything stays at the same electrical level to prevent current flowing through a person.
Explanation B

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
keyIdeas: The use of the mass of earth as the return path and the necessity of an earth electrode.
TT systems rely on local electrodes rather than a supplier-provided terminal, changing how we test and protect the circuit.

In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for Terre-Terre (the French word for Earth). The first letter 'T' tells us the transformer at the substation is connected directly to the earth. The second letter 'T' tells us the consumer's installation is connected to earth via a local Earth Electrode.

An Earth Electrode is usually a metal rod driven deep into the ground outside the property. In a TT system, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal. Instead, the fault current must travel through the mass of the earth itself to get back to the transformer.

Because soil and rock have higher resistance than copper wire, the fault current in a TT system is often quite low. Because of this, TT systems almost always require an RCD to ensure the power is cut quickly, as a standard fuse or circuit breaker might not detect the small fault current.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters and supply paths.
keyIdeas: Separate (S) vs. Combined (C) neutral and protective conductors in the supply.
The system type dictates the Earth Loop Impedance values and the type of equipment used at the intake.
TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide a high-quality earth return path. 'T' means the source is earthed, and 'N' means the consumer's earth is connected to the Neutral point of the supply.

In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for Separate. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are separate wires all the way from the transformer to the property. Usually, the earth is the lead sheath or a separate core of the supply cable.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for Combined and the 'S' stands for Separate. In the street, the Neutral and Earth functions are Combined into one single wire (called a PEN conductor). However, once the cable reaches the consumer's house, they are Separated at the intake. This is the most common system for new installations in the UK and is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME).

steps.title: CPC vs. Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
keyIdeas: The location of the conductor: the Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the means of earthing; the CPC protects individual circuits.
Using the wrong term during inspection and testing leads to failed reports and technical errors.

It is vital to use the correct names for different green-and-yellow conductors. A Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that protects an individual circuit. For example, the earth wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable for a kitchen socket circuit is a CPC. Its job is to link the metal parts of that specific circuit back to the consumer unit.

The Earthing Conductor is the single 'master' wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing. This means it connects the consumer unit to the supplier's earth terminal or to an earth rod.

To remember the difference: the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house, while a CPC is just for one specific circuit. You might have twenty CPCs in a house, but usually only one main Earthing Conductor.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and Components
steps.objective: Identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation based on a provided diagram.
keyIdeas: Identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation based on a provided diagram.
Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are inspecting a small domestic garage conversion. You notice a single concentric supply cable entering the building. At the service head (the cutout), the supplier has provided a single terminal where both the neutral and the earth are connected together. From this point, two separate wires—a blue neutral and a green/yellow earth—run into the consumer unit. You also see a 10mm² green/yellow cable clamped to the copper water main where it enters the building.

Step 1: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a 'Combined' then 'Separate' arrangement. Using our letter codes, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).

Step 2: Identify the main link to the earth source. The green/yellow wire running from the supplier's terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit is the Earthing Conductor. This is the 'master' link for the whole board.

Step 3: Identify the pipe connection. The cable connected to the incoming water main is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. Its job is to ensure the metal pipework stays at the same potential as the electrical earth.

You also see a 10mm² green/yellow cable clamped to the copper water main where it enters the building.

Step 3: Identify the pipe connection. The cable connected to the incoming water main is the Main Protective Bonding Conductor. Its job is to ensure the metal pipework stays at the same potential as the electrical earth.
Step 4: Identify the circuit-level protection. Inside the garage, the green/yellow wire found within the 'twin and earth' cable feeding the lights is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).

steps: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a 'Combined' then 'Separate' arrangement. Using our letter codes, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).

Identify the main link to the earth source. The green/yellow wire running from the supplier's terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) inside the consumer unit is the Earthing Conductor. This is the 'master' link for the whole board.

Identify the circuit-level protection. Inside the garage, the green/yellow wire found within the 'twin and earth' cable feeding the lights is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).
taskSkeleton.takeaway: Always look at the 'intake' (where the power enters) to determine the system type. If neutral and earth share a terminal at the cutout but split before the meter, it is TN-C-S. Don't confuse the Earthing Conductor (the main link) with the CPC (the circuit link).

steps.hint: Focus on the letters: C stands for Combined (in the street) and S stands for Separate (in the house).

steps.title: Integrative Close: Why TT Uses an RCD
steps.objective: Explain why TT systems usually need an RCD.
keyIdeas: TT systems rely on soil for the return path, which has much higher resistance than a metal return path.
High earth path resistance limits fault current, so a fuse or MCB may not trip quickly enough.
An RCD is used because it trips on imbalance rather than needing a large fault current.
anchorFacts: High soil resistance means low fault current in a TT system.
An RCD can disconnect even when the fault current is too small for a fuse or MCB to operate quickly.
taskConstraints: Keep the response to one short explanation.
Require the learner to link high resistance to low fault current and then to RCD operation.

steps.questionText: Using one short explanation, explain why TT systems usually require an RCD.

steps.hint: The key issue is not the label TT by itself; it is the resistance of the earth return path.

spacedReview.title: Spaced Review
spacedReview.objective: Recall the fundamental purpose of overcurrent protective devices and the nature of an earth fault from unit 203-3C.
retrievalTextLines: An overcurrent is any current exceeding the rated capacity of the conductor.
A short circuit occurs between live conductors (Line to Neutral).
An earth fault occurs when a live conductor makes contact with the metallic casing of equipment or the protective conductor.
Protective devices like MCBs and fuses are rated by their In (nominal current) to protect cables from damage.
spacedReview.questionText: In the event of a fault to earth, what is the specific technical role of the protective device (MCB or fuse) regarding the supply?
answerGuidance: The device must automatically disconnect the supply.
It must operate within a specific time to prevent danger.
It breaks the circuit to stop the flow of fault current.

    {
      "id": "203-4a-203-4a-worked-example-1",
      "sourceBlockId": "203-4A-203-4a-worked-example-1",
      "title": "Worked Example: Identifying Earthing Systems and Components",
      "role": "worked_example",
      "stage": "worked_example",
      "progressionRule": "worked_example_feedback",
      "nextStepId": "203-4a-203-4a-guided-practice-1",
      "objective": "Identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation based on a provided diagram.",
      "completionMode": "respond",
      "keyTerms": [
        "TN-C-S System",
        "Earthing Conductor",
        "Main Protective Bonding",
        "TN-C-S",
        "PME",
        "MET"
      ],
      "keyIdeas": [
        "Identify the earthing system type and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation based on a provided diagram.",
        "Show one worked example.",
        "Scenario: You are inspecting a small domestic garage conversion. You notice a single concentric supply cable entering the building. At the service head (the cutout), the supplier has provided a single terminal where both the neutral and the earth are connected together. From this point, two separate wires—a blue neutral and a green/yellow earth—run into the consumer unit. You also see a 10mm² green/yellow cable clamped to the copper water main where it enters the building.",
        "Step 1: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the intake point, this is a 'Combined' then 'Separate' arrangement. Using our letter codes, this is a TN-C-S system (often called PME).",

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
keyIdeas: Identify the three primary earthing system arrangements (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
The role of protective devices and the basic requirement for a low-impedance path.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to installation.
The specific application of bonding conductors in an installation.
Incorrectly identifying bonding types leads to non-compliant and potentially dangerous installations.
anchorFacts: To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
Why this matters: ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to installation.
TT earthing systems
TN-S earthing systems
TN-C-S earthing systems
Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC)
